fuzzy_prd: Hey team, we need to wire up the OpenID Connect auth flow for our platform. Basically we want something similar to what the identity team built for the SSO login module a while back — you know the one that handles the provider redirects and session stuff. The core ask is: when a user hits sign-in, we redirect them to the provider correctly; when they come back from the provider, we validate everything before letting them through; and when they log out, we either kick them to the provider's logout page or just continue normally if that's not available. We also need to expose profile info (name, email, that kind of thing) and token details in a consistent way so downstream code doesn't have to care about provider-specific claim names. Oh and there's the key selection thing for verifying tokens — it needs to handle the different flavors we use. The failure cases are really important here, bad state or missing tokens should produce a predictable redirect so the frontend knows what to do. One thing I keep hearing from ops is that the defaults should just work out of the box without anyone having to configure every single endpoint manually. Can someone pick this up and make sure the output format matches what the test harness expects? Check how we did it before for the exact field names.

trigger_keywords: what are the default endpoints
default provider configuration values
which ports and URLs are used by default
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The default client settings are: scheme=https, port=443, authorization_endpoint=/authorize, token_endpoint=/token, userinfo_endpoint=/userinfo, jwks_uri=/jwk. These are reported as key=value lines when the flow is 'client_defaults'.

trigger_keywords: how is the authorization redirect built
what fields appear in the sign-in redirect output
what does the authorization request flow return
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The authorization redirect output must include: status=302, authorization_url (using https + issuer host + /authorize), client_id=1234, response_type (default 'code'), scope=openid, optional fields login_hint/ui_locales/claims_locales/response_mode when supplied, state reported as 'generated_32_hex' (normalized), and nonce_length=32. Fields appear in that specific order.

trigger_keywords: how does logout routing work
when does logout redirect to the provider vs continue
what is the output when no end_session_endpoint is available
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: If discovery data includes an end_session_endpoint, the logout flow outputs status=302 and logout_url=<endpoint>, plus post_logout_redirect_uri=<uri> if configured. If no end-session endpoint is available (e.g., discovery is not enabled or not present), the output is status=200 and route=application.

trigger_keywords: what happens when state does not match
how are callback validation failures reported
what is the failure output format for bad callbacks
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Callback validation failures always output status=302, a failure_url in the form /auth/failure?message=<msg>&strategy=openid_connect, and an error= field. Invalid state yields error=invalid_callback and detail=invalid_state with message=invalid_credentials. Missing code (response_type=code) yields error=missing_authorization_code with message=missing_code. Missing id_token (response_type=id_token) yields error=missing_identity_token with message=missing_id_token. No exception class names or runtime messages may appear.

trigger_keywords: what does a successful identity token callback return
how is authenticated identity exposed after valid callback
what fields are in the callback success output
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: A successful identity-token callback (response_type=id_token, matching state) outputs: status=200, provider=openid_connect, uid=<sub claim>, name=<name claim>, email=<email claim>. The decoded_id_token is the source of these claims.

trigger_keywords: how are profile claims mapped
what field names does the profile output use
how do provider claim names translate to application fields
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Profile claims mapping (flow=profile_claims) outputs: uid=<sub>, name=<name>, email=<email>, nickname=<preferred_username>, first_name=<given_name>, last_name=<family_name>, gender=<gender>, image=<picture>, phone=<phone_number>, website=<website>, raw_sub=<sub>. Note 'picture' maps to 'image' and 'phone_number' maps to 'phone'.

trigger_keywords: how are credential token values exposed
what does the credential_values flow output
how is the access token field named in output
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Credential values mapping (flow=credential_values) outputs: id_token=<id_token>, token=<access_token> (note: access_token maps to field named 'token'), refresh_token=<refresh_token>, expires_in=<expires_in>, scope=<scope>.

trigger_keywords: how is the verification key selected
what key types are supported for token verification
how does the system distinguish JWK sets from certificates or secrets
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The verification_key flow selects key material based on signing config: if jwk_json containing a 'keys' array is present, output is key_kind=jwk_set; if certificate_pem is present (X.509), output is key_kind=x509_rsa_public_key; if algorithm is HS256 (HMAC), output is key_kind=shared_secret.

trigger_keywords: how is state value represented in test output
why does state show as generated_32_hex
how is nonce reported in the authorization redirect output
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Because state is randomly generated per request, the test contract normalizes it: the state field is always rendered as the literal string 'generated_32_hex' in output. The nonce is not printed directly; instead nonce_length=32 is output to prove generation occurred without exposing the actual value.

trigger_keywords: what is the client_id used in tests
what default client_id is set
where does client_id 1234 come from
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The default client_id used in the authorization redirect is '1234'. This is the hardcoded default client identifier used when no override is supplied in the options.

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
single file or multiple files
project structure
directory layout
should I split into multiple files
monolithic or modular
how complex is the codebase
what files should I create
how to structure the implementation
should I separate business logic from IO
decouple core from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
SOLID principles
separation of concerns
adapter pattern
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: how does the dispatcher work
how is the program invoked
stdin stdout interface
execution adapter
how to read input
how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

trigger_keywords: how to write test.sh
test harness scaffold
automated test script
how should test.sh work
cases-dir parameter
where does test output go
output file naming format
how are test outputs named
rcb_tests structure
test scaffold deliverable
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Yes, public test cases are provided, one file per feature. Ask me for a specific feature by name and I will give you the full test case JSON.

context_pointers.vague_reference: the compatibility logic from that login module we did last time
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The callback validation logic in the callback_request flow handler, specifically state/nonce matching and response_type-conditional code/id_token presence checks described in feature4_callback_validation.json and feature5_identity_token_callback.json

context_pointers.vague_reference: Check how we did it before for the exact field names
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The key=value stdout line format specified across all feature test cases, particularly the claim-to-field name mappings in feature6_profile_and_credentials.json (e.g., 'picture' -> 'image', 'access_token' -> 'token', 'phone_number' -> 'phone')

context_pointers.vague_reference: the different flavors we use for key verification
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The three verification key kinds in feature7_verification_key_selection.json: jwk_set (JWK set JSON with 'keys' array), x509_rsa_public_key (PEM certificate), and shared_secret (HMAC algorithm like HS256 with client secret)

The JSON-driven stdin/stdout adapter contract: each test case provides a JSON object on stdin with a 'flow' field and the program must write the exact key=value newline-separated string to stdout matching expected_output

oracle_data.fallback_response: I don't have specific details on that aspect documented. Please check the test case files under rcb_tests/public_test_cases/ for the exact expected output format, and cross-reference with the feature descriptions in start.md to understand the intended behavior for that flow.

feature1_provider_endpoint_defaults.json.description: Reports the default network endpoints and transport settings used when no provider overrides are supplied.

scheme=https
port=443
authorization_endpoint=/authorize
token_endpoint=/token
userinfo_endpoint=/userinfo
jwks_uri=/jwk

feature2_authorization_redirect.json.description: Builds an authorization redirect for sign-in, preserving protocol parameters and externally visible redirect metadata.

status=302
authorization_url=https://example.com/authorize
client_id=1234
response_type=code
scope=openid
state=generated_32_hex
nonce_length=32

authorization_url=https://example.com/authorize
client_id=1234
response_type=code
scope=openid
login_hint=john.doe@example.com
ui_locales=en
claims_locales=es
state=generated_32_hex
nonce_length=32

status=302
authorization_url=https://example.com/authorize
client_id=1234
response_type=id_token
response_mode=form_post
scope=openid
state=generated_32_hex
nonce_length=32

feature3_logout_routing.json.description: Handles sign-out paths by redirecting to a discovered end-session endpoint when one exists, otherwise forwarding the request to the downstream application.

feature4_callback_validation.json.description: Validates sign-in callback parameters and renders framework-observable failure redirects with normalized error categories for invalid or incomplete callbacks.

feature5_identity_token_callback.json.description: Accepts an identity-token callback when state and nonce are valid, verifies the decoded token, and exposes the authenticated identity to the downstream application.

feature6_profile_and_credentials.json.description: Maps provider claims and issued token values into externally visible account profile and credential fields.

cases.expected_output:
uid=subject-123
name=Jane Doe
email=jane@example.com
nickname=jane
first_name=Jane
last_name=Doe
gender=female
image=https://cdn.example.com/jane.png
phone=+1-555-0100
website=https://jane.example.com
raw_sub=subject-123

feature7_verification_key_selection.json.description: Selects the token verification material based on signing configuration, distinguishing JWK sets, single JWKs, X.509 public keys, and shared secrets.
